name: pytype (Python Type Inference by Google)

performance.notes: Significantly slower than mypy — full inference is expensive

agent_readiness.notes: Google's Python type inference tool — works with unannotated code. pytype src/. pytype -V 3.11 myfile.py. pytype --keep-going. Generated stubs: pytype --output-dir .pytype. .pytype/pyi/ contains inferred type stubs. mergecheck: pytype --merge. merge-pyi to apply generated types back to source. pytype.cfg configuration file. Works with: mypy, pyright (as upstream stub generator). Unlike mypy, doesn't require type annotations — infers types from usage. Catches: attribute errors, wrong argument types, undefined names. Slower than mypy. Better for legacy unannotated code. Generates .pyi stubs from inference. Linux/macOS only.

gotchas: Much slower than mypy — not suitable for tight CI loops
Output errors can be cryptic — less user-friendly than mypy
Less active community development vs mypy/pyright
Some false positives in complex dynamic code
best_when: Legacy Python codebases with no type annotations (inference-based)
When mypy requires too many annotations to add
avoid_when: Well-annotated Python projects (mypy or pyright is better)
Fast CI/CD where type checking speed matters
Most modern Python projects (mypy + pyright dominate)
summary: pytype is Google's Python type inference tool. Works without annotations — infers types from usage. pytype src/; generates .pyi stubs in .pytype/. Apache-2.0 by Google. Linux/macOS only. Much slower than mypy. Best for legacy unannotated codebases or generating type stubs. Modern annotated projects should use mypy or pyright instead. Cryptic errors; less community support.
